_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Calvert County, MD. Established in 1654 as Patuxent County (renamed 1658). Records survive from the 1650s with no catastrophic courthouse fire — one of the best-preserved county record series on the Western Shore.

records_housed: court records from the 19th century forward
historical records at Maryland State Archives

previous_courthouses.location: Calverton (near Battle Creek) — early court sessions at various locations
previous_courthouses.fate: County seat relocated to Prince Frederick by the 1720s.

previous_courthouses.location: Prince Frederick — frame and then brick courthouse
previous_courthouses.fate: During the Civil War, Union forces briefly occupied the county but did not destroy the courthouse. A fire in 1882 damaged the courthouse building but most bound record volumes survived.
Served until the current courthouse was constructed in 1916.

record_losses.cause: Fire damage to the Prince Frederick courthouse
records_destroyed: some loose papers and recent files
record_losses.description: The 1882 fire at the Prince Frederick courthouse caused less damage than the catastrophic fires in neighboring Prince George's County. Most bound volumes survived. The loss was concentrated in unbound papers and recent files.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Calvert County escaped the worst — the bound records that document {ancestor_name}'s land, estate, and court appearances largely survived the 1882 fire.
record_losses.source: Stein, Charles Francis, Jr. A History of Calvert County, Maryland. Baltimore: Privately published, 1960.

deeds.completeness: Very good. Land records survive from 1654 — one of the oldest continuous county deed series in Maryland. Bound volumes weathered the 1882 fire.
deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in the 1650s-1660s. Substantially complete from the 1670s onward.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis; Calvert County Circuit Court, Prince Frederick

wills_estates.completeness: Very good. Will books survive from the 1650s. Colonial-era wills also in the Prerogative Court records at the State Archives. Inventories from the colonial period are detailed.
wills_estates.gaps: Some fragmentary gaps in the earliest decades. Prerogative Court copies provide backup.
wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives; Calvert County Register of Wills

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1777. Earlier marriages tracked through church records (Christ Church, All Saints, Catholic missions).
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; Calvert County Clerk of Court

court_minutes.completeness: Good. Court proceedings survive from the 1650s. Some fragmentation in the earliest decades.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor early gaps. Colonial-era records substantially complete.
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; Archives of Maryland Online

vital_records.repository: Maryland Department of Health; Maryland State Archives

church_records.parish: Christ Church, Port Republic (Anglican/Episcopal); All Saints' Parish (Anglican); Catholic missions served by Jesuits from Charles and St. Mary's counties
church_records.completeness: Anglican parish vestry records from 1692. Catholic records from the Jesuit missions also cover Calvert County families. The Christ Church records are well-preserved.
church_records.repository: Maryland State Archives; Maryland Diocesan Archives

substitute_sources.description: Provincial-level wills, inventories, and administration accounts for Calvert County families. Filed at the Prerogative Court in Annapolis and now held at the Maryland State Archives.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The inventories document household goods, livestock, enslaved workers, and debts in extraordinary detail. Essential for understanding economic status and family composition.
substitute_sources.source: Carr and Walsh, 'Inventories and the Analysis of Wealth.' Historical Methods 13, no. 2 (1980).

substitute_sources.description: The Calvert County Historical Society maintains family files, Bible records, newspaper clippings, and local history materials that supplement the official records.
substitute_sources.repository: Calvert County Historical Society, Prince Frederick
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Family files and Bible records can fill gaps between official record entries. Local history research provides context.
substitute_sources.source: Calvert County Historical Society. Publications and Proceedings, various issues.

narrative_hooks: Calvert County's records survived — no catastrophic fire, no war damage, no deliberate destruction. {ancestor_name}'s deed, will, or court record likely exists in the bound volumes that stretch back to 1654.

The Prerogative Court inventory for {ancestor_name}'s estate may list every item in the household — from the pewter spoons to the tobacco hogsheads to the names of the enslaved workers. Maryland's colonial inventories are among the most detailed in English America.
If {ancestor_name} lived on Calvert County's narrow peninsula, the records in Prince Frederick and Annapolis document a life bounded by the Chesapeake on one side and the Patuxent on the other.
The church records at Christ Church, Port Republic, hold baptisms, marriages, and burials that complement the county court records — two parallel documentation systems for the same families.
source_references: Stein, Charles Francis, Jr. A History of Calvert County, Maryland. Baltimore: Privately published, 1960.
Carr, Lois Green, and Lorena S. Walsh. 'Inventories and the Analysis of Wealth.' Historical Methods 13, no. 2 (1980).
Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
